text:
King's College Hospital 

Department of Neurosciences 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr A. Patel
Consultant Neurologist  
King's College Hospital  
Department of Neurosciences, King's College Hospital, London SE5 9RS  

Dear Dr A. Patel
Consultant Neurologist,  

Daniel Fraser, DOB: 14-03-1986, Hospital No: KCH728511 NHS No. 9476203185  
12 Brookview Court, Dulwich, London SE21 7HT

Thank you for your referral and for the accompanying occupational health summaries from the patient’s employer, which have been helpful collateral sources. I reviewed him in the neurology clinic today regarding his known generalised epilepsy, and we discussed recent changes in seizure control and functional impact at work.

He reports brief myoclonic jerks on some mornings, particularly with sleep curtailment, and intermittent absence episodes noted by colleagues as brief lapses in conversation. Of particular concern, he and his partner describe clustering of generalised tonic–clonic seizures. Cluster burden increased since 07/Dec/2023; now weekly, five per cluster. He typically experiences a prodrome of diffuse anxiety and photophobia the evening before, followed by a series of convulsions overnight into the early morning. Postictally he has diffuse myalgia and marked fatigue lasting 24–48 hours. The employer’s occupational health summaries corroborate recurrent short-notice absences after nocturnal seizure nights and document witnessed brief staring spells during daytime meetings.

He has a history of generalised epilepsy since late adolescence, previously well controlled for several years. No known focal auras, and no clear lateralising motor features have been observed by his partner. Prior EEGs reportedly showed generalised spike–wave activity; brain MRI was normal by prior account. He denies recent head injury, alcohol excess, or recreational drug use. Sleep restriction and high-intensity screen use late at night appear to be consistent precipitants. There is a family history of seizures in a maternal uncle.

Current anti-seizure medication is valproate 1,000 mg twice daily, which he has taken for many years with good prior efficacy and tolerability other than weight gain. He reports full adherence. He does not currently drive and understands DVLA regulations pertaining to seizures. No recent medication changes at work. No features suggestive of dissociative events were described, and there has been no psychosis or prolonged confusion outside the immediate postictal period.

On examination today he was alert and oriented with normal language, cranial nerves, coordination, tone and power. No focal neurological deficits were elicited. Cardiovascular and general examination were unremarkable.

In summary, this patient has generalised epilepsy with recent deterioration characterised by weekly clustering of generalised tonic–clonic seizures, with five seizures per cluster since early December 2023, alongside morning myoclonus and occasional absences. The pattern and collateral documentation from employer occupational health support a genuine increase in seizure burden and associated occupational impact.

I have arranged for updated EEG with sleep deprivation and ambulatory capture if feasible, and I will request retrieval of prior EEG/MRI reports for comparison. We discussed lifestyle measures including sleep regularity, hydration, and minimising late-evening screen exposure; he is motivated to implement these. I will review the results promptly and consider rational optimisation of therapy in line with generalised epilepsy management, taking into account his prior good response to valproate and current side-effect profile. I have provided a clinic contact pathway for interim concerns. Occupational health may continue to supply periodic summaries, which remain useful collateral sources for monitoring functional outcomes and attendance patterns.

Thank you for your ongoing care. Please let me know if there is further information from primary care or occupational health that would assist.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: Cluster burden increased since 07/Dec/2023; now weekly, five per cluster

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: Identify explicit numeric seizure frequencies. The letter states: 'clustering of generalised tonic–clonic seizures. Cluster burden increased since 07/Dec/2023; now weekly, five per cluster.' This provides a clear cluster frequency (weekly) and number of seizures per cluster (five). Other seizure types mentioned (brief myoclonic jerks on some mornings; intermittent absence episodes) do not have explicit numeric frequencies. Rule for multiple frequencies: select the highest frequency, but since only the clusters have explicit numeric data, we report the cluster format. Normalize to allowed format: '<value1> cluster per <value2> <unit>, <value3> per cluster' becomes '1 cluster per week, 5 per cluster'.
reference: Cluster burden increased since 07/Dec/2023; now weekly, five per cluster
